attributes.stem:
A 57-year-old woman complains that for the last 18 months her right hand has been shaking. The shaking is worse when she tries to use her hand and is not present at rest. Her writing has become "very shaky" and she has great difficulty filling in forms. As a musical instrumentalist, she also has a history of performance anxiety, which leads to palpitations and trembling hands, making it difficult to play her instrument with precision. She does not experience any anxiety at other times and her symptoms are improved following a glass of red wine. She does not take any regular medications.

On examination her blood pressure is 132/80 mmHg, pulse 75/min, respiratory rate 13/min, oxygen saturation 99% and temperature 36.9°C. She has no tremor at rest, but when she holds both her hands up there is a bilateral fine tremor, augmented by clenching her fists. The remaining examination of the nervous system is normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has an essential tremor (ET). The major indication for medical therapy of essential tremor is interference with activities of daily living. When the patient's symptoms are such that some form of treatment is required, a non-selective beta blocker (such as propranolol) is the first category of drugs to use. While no major guideline has specifically addressed the treatment of performance anxiety or stage fright, propranolol is frequently used off-label to alleviate such situational anxiety, and there is evidence demonstrating its effectiveness in reducing fear and enhancing performance.

One differential diagnosis for essential tremor is Parkinsonian tremor/Parkinson's disease (PD).

  • ET: Postural and action tremor, bilateral, normal muscle tone, tremor often improves with alcohol

  • PD: Resting tremor, unilateral in the early stage, possible cogwheel and/or leadpipe rigidity, no improvement of tremor with alcohol

Selegiline is a monoamine oxidase type B inhibitor which has shown some effect in the management of tremor associated with Parkinson's disease if response to levodopa or anticholinergic drugs is insufficient. This is because monoamine oxidases (MAOs) metabolise dopamine, thus inhibition of MAOs augments dopamine levels and actions.

Levodopa is a precursor to dopamine and is thus used for dopamine replacement therapy. Carbidopa (a decarboxylase inhibitor), is given in combination with levodopa, where it inhibits the peripheral metabolism of levodopa thus making it more readily available in the brain. Levodopa/carbidopa is first line in the management of tremor associated with Parkinson's disease.

Primidone is a barbiturate which is second-line to beta blockers in the management of essential tremor. This is through its actions on sodium and calcium channel transport whereby it reduces frequency of nerve firing. This class of drug is poorly tolerated by patients in comparison to beta-blockers (especially in the elderly as it is particularly sedating). Occasionally, the patient needs propranolol and primidone combined.

Benzhexol is also known as trihexyphenidyl and is an anticholinergic mediation, which can be used to manage hypertonicity associated with Parkinson's disease, frequently referred to as 'cogwheel rigidity'.
learningPoints: A non-selective beta blocker is the preferred treatment for essential tremor.
Essential tremor is characterised by a postural and action tremor that is typically bilateral, with normal muscle tone, and often shows improvement with alcohol consumption.
references: eTG by Therapeutic Guidelines. 2017. Essential Tremor. [online] Available from: https://tgldcdp.tg.org.au/viewTopic?etgAccess=true&guidelinePage=Neurology&topicfile=essential-tremor&guidelinename=Neurology&sectionId=toc_d1e467#toc_d1e467

Evidence-based guideline update: treatment of essential tremor: report of the Quality Standards subcommittee of the American Academy of Neurology. Neurology. 2011 Nov 8;77(19):1752-5. https://n.neurology.org/content/77/19/1752

Szeleszczuk Ł, Frączkowski D. Propranolol versus other selected drugs in the treatment of various types of anxiety or stress, with particular reference to stage fright and post-traumatic stress disorder. International Journal of Molecular Sciences. 2022 Sep 3;23(17):10099. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9456064/

Bötzel K, Tronnier V, Gasser T. The differential diagnosis and treatment of tremor. Dtsch Arztebl Int. 2014 Mar 28;111(13):225-35; quiz 236. doi: 10.3238/arztebl.2014.0225. PMID: 24739887; PMCID: PMC3991161.
